structured.summary: The paper introduces LAMOL, a novel method for lifelong language learning using language modeling, which effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting by generating pseudo-samples of previous tasks without requiring additional memory or model capacity.

strengths.point: LAMOL effectively prevents catastrophic forgetting in lifelong language learning.
The method does not require additional memory or model capacity.
LAMOL approaches the multitasking upper bound within 2-3%.
The paper provides open-source code to facilitate further research.

weaknesses.point: The paper lacks a detailed comparison with other state-of-the-art lifelong learning methods.
The evaluation is limited to only five language tasks, which may not fully demonstrate the scalability of LAMOL.
There is no discussion on the computational efficiency or resource requirements of LAMOL.
The impact of the sampling ratio (γ) on performance is not thoroughly analyzed.
The method's performance on tasks with highly imbalanced data is not addressed.
The paper does not provide insights into the limitations or potential failure cases of LAMOL.

questions.question: How does LAMOL perform on a larger and more diverse set of language tasks?
questions.motivation: Understanding scalability and generalization to various NLP tasks is crucial for assessing the method's applicability.

questions.question: What are the computational costs associated with generating pseudo-samples in LAMOL?
questions.motivation: Evaluating the method's efficiency and resource requirements is important for practical deployment.

questions.question: How does the choice of the sampling ratio (γ) affect the performance of LAMOL?
questions.motivation: Clarifying the impact of hyperparameters on the model's performance can guide future research and application.

critique_points.point_001: The paper lacks a detailed comparison with other state-of-the-art lifelong learning methods. (Section 2 Related Work)
critique_points.point_002: The evaluation is limited to only five language tasks, which may not fully demonstrate the scalability of LAMOL. (Abstract)
critique_points.point_003: There is no discussion on the computational efficiency or resource requirements of LAMOL. (Throughout the paper)
critique_points.point_004: The impact of the sampling ratio (γ) on performance is not thoroughly analyzed. (Section 3.2 Training)
critique_points.point_005: The method's performance on tasks with highly imbalanced data is not addressed. (Throughout the paper)
critique_points.point_006: The paper does not provide insights into the limitations or potential failure cases of LAMOL. (Throughout the paper)
critique_points.point_007: How does LAMOL perform on a larger and more diverse set of language tasks? (Understanding scalability and generalization to various NLP tasks is crucial for assessing the method's applicability.)
critique_points.point_008: What are the computational costs associated with generating pseudo-samples in LAMOL? (Evaluating the method's efficiency and resource requirements is important for practical deployment.)
critique_points.point_009: How does the choice of the sampling ratio (γ) affect the performance of LAMOL? (Clarifying the impact of hyperparameters on the model's performance can guide future research and application.)
